metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical verifier-mode enum vocabulary (e.g., `static | jwks | introspection | proxy | custom`) and the exact config key / env-var name that selects among them (e.g., `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_MODE` / `[mcp.auth] mode`) in the epic, so S1–S5 implementors share a single string contract.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a verifier-mode selector (the `[mcp.auth]` config namespace) and names five modes — 'static bearer', 'JWKS/JWT', 'RFC 7662 introspection', 'trusted-proxy passthrough', 'pluggable custom' — but never defines the string vocabulary (the allowed values of the config key that selects among them). An implementor writing `McpConfig` extension, `MCP_ENV_VARS` entries, and the composite dispatcher needs a canonical enum such as `{"static", "jwks", "introspection", "proxy", "custom"}` (or equivalent); without it, S1–S5 child stories will each invent their own token strings and diverge.
findings.location: Scope section ('five verifiers … selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars'); AC line 4 ('all five verifier modes … selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Config schema' subsection to Scope or Approach naming the selector key and its allowed string values, e.g.: `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_MODE ∈ {static, jwks, introspection, proxy, custom}` mapping to each verifier. This becomes the ground truth for `McpConfig` extension, `MCP_ENV_VARS`, and the composite dispatcher.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an integration-level (httpx ASGITransport) test asserting that a bearer token with a wrong audience value is rejected with HTTP 401, exercising the full transport→composite→verifier→response path.
findings.finding: The parent AC and success criteria require 'wrong-audience rejection' to be demonstrated in the local test suite at the same observable level (HTTP status) as the co-listed guarantees (401-without-token, 200-with-valid-token, 403-insufficient-scope, DNS-rebinding 421/403). Across all seven children, wrong-audience rejection is only tested at the unit level (S2: 'unit test asserts... a wrong-audience token is rejected (returns None)'; S3: unit test 'rejection for wrong aud'). No child schedules an integration-level (httpx ASGITransport) assertion that a wrong-audience bearer token produces an HTTP 401 end-to-end through the transport. This is a scope-narrowing partial coverage: the child tests a precursor (the verifier returns None) but not the same observable outcome (HTTP 401 from the live endpoint).
findings.location: child 61e6-6a65-f162-47cf (S2); roster-wide absence confirmed across all siblings
findings.suggested_fix: In S2's integration test block, add a test case: set up the server with static auth enabled (resource_server_url='https://rebar.example.com'), issue a static token configured with aud='https://other.example.com', assert HTTP 401 in response. This mirrors the pattern of the existing 403 (wrong-scope) integration test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S6 should declare S7 as a completion dependency (or move AC4 to S7 as S7's AC), making the cross-story documentation requirement explicit in S6's Scope/Depends-on section.
findings.finding: S6's fourth acceptance criterion creates an undeclared ordering dependency on S7: 'docs/mcp-auth.md (in S7) documents that the import path is a trusted operator config value.' This makes S6 formally incomplete until S7 is delivered. However, S6's Scope section only declares 'Depends on S2. Independent of S3/S4/S5' — S7 is not listed as a dependency or blocker. A reviewer checking S6's own AC checklist cannot mark S6 done until S7 merges, but the dependency is invisible in the story's stated relationships, risking premature sign-off on S6.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add 'Completion of AC4 depends on S7' to S6's Scope section, or (b) move S6's AC4 into S7's acceptance criteria as 'docs/mcp-auth.md documents that the custom-verifier import path is a trusted operator config value, not derived from user input.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S5 must name the specific config key / env var that serves as the 'explicit acknowledgment' for a non-loopback trusted-proxy bind and register it in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json.
findings.finding: S5 states 'loopback bind required when this mode is active (refuse a non-loopback bind without an explicit acknowledgment)' but does not define the name, form, or interface of this 'explicit acknowledgment.' S1 uses a non-empty http_allowed_hosts/http_allowed_origins allowlist as the acknowledgment for non-loopback binds generally. S5 implies a separate, stricter gate for trusted-proxy mode on a non-loopback bind — but whether this is an additional env var (e.g., REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_REMOTE_TRUSTED_PROXY), the same allowlist mechanism, or something else is unspecified. This is a residual reference: a constraint is stated but its interface is left unresolved, creating an implementation ambiguity and a potential gap in the config surface documentation (AC for MCP_ENV_VARS).
findings.location: child 85a6-2fea-e2c9-4819 (S5)
findings.suggested_fix: In S5's What section, add: 'The non-loopback acknowledgment for trusted-proxy mode is a new config key mcp.auth.trusted_proxy_allow_remote_bind (env: REBAR_MCP_TRUSTED_PROXY_ALLOW_REMOTE_BIND, bool, default false); add it to MCP_ENV_VARS and server.json.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required injection mechanism for the static bearer token secret (e.g., mounted secret file or secrets-manager reference) and explicitly prohibit placing it as a plaintext value in docker-compose.yml / IaC env blocks; document this in `docs/mcp-auth.md` and in the child story (S2) that implements the static verifier.
findings.finding: The static bearer verifier requires a secret token at startup, but the plan does not specify the required injection mechanism. The existing rebar deployment pattern is direct env-var values in docker-compose.yml environment blocks (confirmed in infra/compose/docker-compose.yml). An implementer following that pattern could place the raw static bearer token as a plaintext value in docker-compose.yml, a .env file, or a container spec — violating the plan's own stated requirement ('no plaintext secrets in code/IaC/logs on the boundary-crossing path') without any plan-level constraint preventing it.
findings.location: Security section — 'Per-verifier hardening: static = constant-time compare + hash-at-rest'; Scope — 'static bearer' verifier; AC — 'All five verifier modes selectable via [mcp.auth] config / REBAR_MCP_* env vars'
findings.suggested_fix: In the static verifier spec (S2) and docs/mcp-auth.md, require the token to be read from a file path (REBAR_MCP_STATIC_BEARER_TOKEN_FILE) rather than a direct env-var value, or document the mandatory use of Docker/Compose secrets / a secrets manager. Treat a direct-env-var path as an advisory warning at startup ('token supplied via env var; prefer a mounted secret file for production deployments').

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] S5 must specify the exact ASGI/Starlette middleware composition point (e.g., 'wrap mcp.streamable_http_app() before the auth middleware', 'add as a Starlette middleware at position N') and confirm the ordering contract with S1/S2's pipeline.
findings.finding: S5 introduces 'a small ASGI middleware plus a verifier' for the trusted-proxy pattern but does not specify how this ASGI middleware is registered in the application pipeline established by S1 (FastMCP construction with TransportSecuritySettings) and S2 (token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier wired into FastMCP). The SDK's FastMCP accepts a token_verifier parameter for the verifier portion, but ASGI middleware must be composed at a different layer (e.g., wrapping the ASGI app or using a Starlette middleware stack). S5 does not name the integration seam, leaving an unresolved dependency gap between S5's middleware and the S1/S2 construction contract.
findings.location: child 85a6-2fea-e2c9-4819 (S5)
findings.suggested_fix: In S5's Approach section, add: 'The header-guard middleware is composed as an outermost ASGI wrapper around the app returned by mcp.streamable_http_app() (or equivalent), so it runs before the SDK auth middleware strips/validates the Authorization header — ensuring client-supplied identity headers are removed before any downstream trust decision.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Each of S3, S4, S5, and S6 should explicitly state that it will update MCP_ENV_VARS and server.json with its own verifier-specific REBAR_MCP_* params and that scripts/check_server_manifest.py passes as part of its own AC.
findings.finding: S3, S4, S5, and S6 each introduce new verifier-specific config parameters (e.g., REBAR_MCP_JWKS_URI, REBAR_MCP_INTROSPECTION_ENDPOINT, REBAR_MCP_PROXY_SECRET, REBAR_MCP_CUSTOM_VERIFIER) that must be registered in MCP_ENV_VARS and server.json for the CI drift gate to pass, yet none of these four children explicitly states it will update MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json for its own params. Only S1 (transport keys), S2 (auth framework keys), and S7 ('finalize server.json') explicitly own manifest updates. Each child's own 'make lint && make typecheck && make test pass' AC includes the drift gate, so an unregistered key would cause those children's own tests to fail — but the ownership is unspecified, leaving it unclear which story performs each update. This is a scope-narrowing partial coverage of parent AC6 and creates an owned-by-none gap for verifier-specific env vars in S3–S6.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to each of S3–S6: an AC line reading 'New REBAR_MCP_* env vars introduced by this story are added to MCP_ENV_VARS and server.json; the CI drift gate (scripts/check_server_manifest.py) passes.' S7 then regenerates docs/mcp-reference.md and docs/env-vars.md from the already-updated manifest.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a startup warning (logged at WARNING level, non-fatal) when the server binds to a non-loopback interface without a configured TLS cert/proxy hint, and require `docs/mcp-auth.md` to include an explicit section on TLS requirements for non-loopback deployments.
findings.finding: The plan declares 'TLS is assumed terminated at the edge' but provides no startup-time enforcement or warning when the server is bound to a non-loopback interface without any detectable TLS. An operator who configures a non-loopback bind (which the plan explicitly supports) but misconfigures or omits edge TLS would serve bearer tokens in plaintext over an untrusted network, with no rebar-side signal that this is happening. The docs deliverable (docs/mcp-auth.md) is required but the plan does not mandate that it must include a TLS warning for non-loopback deployments.
findings.location: Security section — 'TLS is assumed terminated at the edge (bearer tokens never in query strings, never logged)'; S1 — HTTP transport + transport hardening; docs/mcp-auth.md requirement
findings.suggested_fix: In S1 (HTTP transport hardening), add a startup check: if `host` is not a loopback address and no `REBAR_MCP_TLS_TERMINATOR_ACKNOWLEDGED=1` flag is set, emit a `WARNING` log line stating 'rebar-mcp is bound to a non-loopback interface over plain HTTP; ensure TLS is terminated at the edge before bearer tokens are exchanged.' This is advisory (non-fatal) but surfaces the misconfiguration at the earliest opportunity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly assign to a child story) that the introspection and trusted-proxy verifiers do not echo or forward the inbound bearer token to any upstream, verified by a unit test asserting the upstream call uses only the verifier's own credentials.
findings.finding: The Security section states 'No token passthrough to any upstream (confused-deputy prohibition)' as a hard requirement, but no Acceptance Criterion or test oracle verifies this property. It is a described security deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Security section ('No token passthrough to any upstream (confused-deputy prohibition)') vs Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `pyproject.toml` `[mcp]` extra and `dev` group pins from `mcp>=1.9` to `mcp>=1.28,<2` before child stories begin, and add a smoke-import test that confirms `AuthSettings`, `streamable_http_app`, and `TransportSecuritySettings` are importable, to lock the floor against regression.
findings.finding: The plan's four load-bearing FastMCP capability assertions — `FastMCP(token_verifier=..., auth=AuthSettings(...))`, `mcp.streamable_http_app()`, `TransportSecuritySettings`, and `FastMCP.run(transport='streamable-http')` — are all UNRESOLVED in the installed SDK (`mcp>=1.9`). The plan states these were verified against mcp v1.28.1 and calls for pinning `mcp>=1.28,<2`, but pyproject.toml still declares `mcp>=1.9` for both the `[mcp]` extra and the `dev` group. Child stories could begin against a >=1.9-satisfying install where the entire auth API surface is absent.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Compatibility & rollback section ('pinning mcp>=1.28,<2'); pyproject.toml lines 82 and 149 (`mcp>=1.9`)
findings.suggested_fix: In `pyproject.toml`, change both occurrences of `"mcp>=1.9"` to `"mcp>=1.28,<2"`. In S1, add a module-level smoke test: `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP, AuthSettings, TransportSecuritySettings` and `from mcp import streamable_http_app` (or whatever the correct import path is at 1.28.1, confirmed empirically) so the CI gate enforces the floor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the config namespace: either update Success Criteria to say 'a new `[mcp.auth]` sub-table within the `[mcp]` config surface' or update the AC to say 'selectable via new keys in `[mcp]` config', so both sections describe the same structure.
findings.finding: Success Criteria says the capability is 'configured entirely through the existing `[mcp]` config/env surface,' implying no new config namespace, while the AC says verifiers are 'selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config' — two different implementers could reasonably interpret these as (a) new keys added flat into `[mcp]` vs (b) a new `[mcp.auth]` TOML sub-table. If `[mcp.auth]` is genuinely a new subsection, the Success Criteria description is misleading; if it is not, the AC is misleading.
findings.location: Success Criteria (first bullet) vs Acceptance Criteria ('selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config')
findings.suggested_fix: Change Success Criteria first bullet to: '…configured through a new `[mcp.auth]` sub-table and `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars within the existing `[mcp]` config surface…'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out step for the `mcp>=1.28,<2` / `pyjwt[crypto]` dependency pin changes (e.g., note they land in an isolated commit, revert instructions, or document the prior bounds) so the rollback section covers both behavioral and dependency rollback.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback mechanism ('leave the new keys unset / mcp.transport=stdio') addresses only behavioral rollback and does not cover rolling back the dependency-version change — tightening `mcp` from its current lower bound to `>=1.28` is a change to the build manifest that, once merged, cannot be undone by config. No explicit back-out plan (e.g., a separate commit that reverts pyproject.toml, or a note that the pin is applied in a dedicated commit for easy revert) is provided.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section; 'The one dependency change is pinning mcp>=1.28,<2'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Compatibility & rollback' section, add: 'The dependency pin is applied in a standalone commit (e.g., `chore: pin mcp>=1.28,<2 and add pyjwt[crypto]`). To roll back the pin independently of behavior, revert that commit; no application-layer changes are required alongside it.' Also record the current (pre-epic) mcp lower bound so the revert is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default HTTP port (or state 'port is always caller-supplied / no default, must be set via `REBAR_MCP_HTTP_PORT`') so executor and docs agree without a design decision at implementation time.
findings.finding: The default HTTP port for the Streamable-HTTP transport is not stated in the plan. The plan specifies 'loopback bind by default' for the IP but omits the port. An executor implementing the transport and writing operational docs must pick a port, and different executors might choose differently (8000, 9000, a random ephemeral port, etc.), creating divergence between implementation and docs.
findings.location: Security section ('loopback bind by default') and Scope section ('optional Streamable-HTTP transport')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope or Compatibility section: 'The HTTP transport binds to `127.0.0.1:8000` by default (overridable via `REBAR_MCP_HTTP_PORT` / `[mcp] http_port`); no default is baked in for non-loopback binds.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the custom verifier loading mechanism (e.g., 'Python dotted import path in `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_CUSTOM_VERIFIER` evaluated at startup', or 'Python-API-only seam, not config-selectable, exempted from the env-var AC') so the executor can implement and test it without a design decision.
findings.finding: The 'custom' (pluggable) verifier mode does not specify how a caller-supplied verifier class is registered at runtime. The plan names it as one of five selectable verifier modes and requires it to be 'selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars', but gives no indication of the loading mechanism (Python dotted import path in config? entry-point group? direct Python API only?). An executor implementing this verifier mode must make a non-trivial design choice that affects both the config schema and the security boundary.
findings.location: Scope section ('five verifiers … pluggable custom'); Acceptance Criteria ('All five verifier modes … selectable via `[mcp.auth]` config / `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies: (a) when no bind address is configured the server binds to loopback only, and (b) attempting a non-loopback bind without an explicit host/origin allowlist fails fast (or is blocked by `TransportSecuritySettings`).
findings.finding: The Security section specifies two testable transport-hardening behaviors — (1) loopback bind by default, and (2) non-loopback bind requires an explicit host/origin allowlist — but no Acceptance Criterion covers these behaviors. They are described deliverables with no verification oracle.
findings.location: Security section ('loopback bind by default, explicit host/origin allowlist required for a non-loopback bind') vs Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the new ADR (and in the epic's doc plan) whether it supersedes or amends ADR-0007, and add a cross-reference or 'superseded-by' header to ADR-0007 as part of the epic's documentation deliverables.
findings.finding: ADR-0007 is cited as the architectural record establishing that MCP is stdio-only and that remote access needs are met by separate FastAPI services (review_bot, op-cert). This epic adds HTTP transport directly to rebar-mcp, materially changing that architectural constraint. The plan promises a new ADR but does not state it will supersede or amend ADR-0007, leaving ADR-0007 as a potentially stale and contradictory reference.
findings.location: Context section ('per ADR-0007'); Acceptance Criteria — 'docs/mcp-auth.md and an ADR document…'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Acceptance Criteria doc bullet, add: 'The new ADR includes a `Supersedes: ADR-0007` (or `Amends: ADR-0007`) header, and ADR-0007 gains a `Superseded-by`/`Amended-by` note pointing to the new ADR.' This keeps the ADR chain navigable and prevents stale guidance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how McpConfig is extended to support auth sub-config (either a nested McpAuthConfig sub-dataclass with corresponding coerce_sparse/from_mapping changes, or flat mcp.auth_* keys in McpConfig) so the [mcp.auth] config surface has a concrete schema-layer mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan specifies new auth config under '[mcp.auth]' as a TOML sub-table, but the existing McpConfig is a flat dataclass and the config system's coerce_sparse/_SECTION_CLASSES infrastructure handles only top-level sections — it has no sub-section dispatch mechanism for keys nested within an existing section. Adding a [mcp.auth] sub-table requires either a new McpAuthConfig sub-dataclass with changes to McpConfig.from_mapping/coerce_sparse, or switching to flat 'mcp.*' keys — but the plan does not specify which approach is chosen or how the schema extension is accomplished.
findings.location: Scope section ('config/manifest updates (McpConfig, MCP_ENV_VARS, server.json)'); AC line 'All five verifier modes … selectable via [mcp.auth] config / REBAR_MCP_* env vars'
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend adding a nested `McpAuthConfig` sub-dataclass and extending `McpConfig.from_mapping` to parse the `[mcp.auth]` sub-table — consistent with how `[tool.rebar.mcp]` is a sub-table of `[tool.rebar]`. Document this explicitly in S1 or S2 as a config-schema task.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the composite TokenVerifier's dispatch semantics (e.g., exactly-one active verifier selected by config, or ordered-chain with fail-closed on any-None) so the implementation is unambiguous and consistent with the fail-closed security posture.
findings.finding: The composite TokenVerifier's dispatch semantics (first-match, OR-logic, sequential fallback) are unspecified, yet the ordering matters for correctness: the trusted-proxy verifier must strip client-supplied identity headers before other verifiers run, and a misconfigured composite could silently fall through to a less-strict verifier on failure. The plan names 'composite TokenVerifier + config-driven selection' without specifying whether multiple verifiers can be active simultaneously and, if so, how the composite routes requests.
findings.location: Approach & rationale: 'composite TokenVerifier + config-driven selection'; Security section: 'trusted-proxy = loopback + shared-secret + strip client identity headers'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign the mcp>=1.28,<2 pin update (in both the [mcp] extra and [dev] in pyproject.toml) to S1 as a hard prerequisite, and add an AC asserting the pin is in place before S2 begins.
findings.finding: The plan mandates pinning 'mcp>=1.28,<2' to access TokenVerifier, AuthSettings, and the Streamable-HTTP transport, but the current pyproject.toml has 'mcp>=1.9' — a lower bound that does not guarantee those APIs exist. The pin change is only implicitly assigned to S1; if a child story (S2–S6) is developed or reviewed against an environment satisfying mcp>=1.9 but not >=1.28, the TokenVerifier import will fail at runtime rather than at dependency resolution time, producing a confusing implementation gap.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback: 'The one dependency change is pinning mcp>=1.28,<2'; pyproject.toml line 82: 'mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]'
findings.suggested_fix: In S1's AC or definition of done, add: 'pyproject.toml [mcp] extra and [dev] both updated to mcp>=1.28,<2; mcp.server.auth.provider.TokenVerifier and mcp.server.auth.settings.AuthSettings importable in CI.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit compile-time import smoke-test (e.g., a 2-line `assert hasattr(FastMCP(...), 'streamable_http_app')` and `from mcp.server.auth.settings import AuthSettings; from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP; FastMCP.__init__.__doc__` check) to S1's acceptance criteria or a pre-S1 spike task, so the exact kwarg names (`token_verifier`, `auth`, `transport_security`) and `streamable_http_app()` availability are confirmed against the pinned mcp 1.28.x before S1 implementation begins.
findings.finding: The plan names `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…), host=…, port=…, transport_security=TransportSecuritySettings(…))` and `mcp.streamable_http_app()` as central API surfaces, but `TransportSecuritySettings`, the `token_verifier`/`auth` constructor kwargs on `FastMCP`, and `streamable_http_app()` are UNRESOLVED at symbol level in the installed environment — they cannot be confirmed by import resolution alone. The design-of-record session log grounds these as investigated at mcp v1.28.1, but no child story is required to emit a compile-time import smoke-test before S1 commits to the full implementation.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / Testing strategy / S1 (implicit)
findings.suggested_fix: In S1's AC, add: 'A `scripts/verify_mcp_sdk_surface.py` script (or inline pytest fixture) confirms `from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP; from mcp.server.auth.settings import AuthSettings; from mcp.server.auth.provider import TokenVerifier, AccessToken` import cleanly and that `FastMCP` accepts `token_verifier` and `auth` kwargs (inspect `FastMCP.__init__` signature). Run this as the first CI step after pinning `mcp>=1.28,<2`.' This is a 5-minute guard that gates the rest of the epic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC line requiring that `pyproject.toml` `mcp` optional-extra is updated to `mcp>=1.28,<2` and includes `pyjwt[crypto]`, and that a clean `pip install 'nava-rebar[mcp]'` from the pinned floor resolves both deps correctly (verifiable via `pip install --dry-run` or a CI tox/venv job).
findings.finding: The plan proposes bumping the `mcp` optional-extra floor from `mcp>=1.9` (current `pyproject.toml`) to `mcp>=1.28,<2`, and separately adding `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `mcp` extra. Neither update appears in any AC line — the AC only says the CI manifest drift gate (`check_server_manifest.py`) passes, but that gate checks `server.json`, not `pyproject.toml` correctness. If a child story omits the `pyproject.toml` update, the new auth code ships but a fresh `pip install 'nava-rebar[mcp]'` still resolves mcp 1.9 (missing `TokenVerifier`, `AuthSettings`) and has no `pyjwt`.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback / Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the composition/routing protocol for the `TokenVerifier` dispatcher: state whether the mode selector is exclusive (one active verifier) or allows chaining, and define what the dispatcher does on `None` return vs. exception from a verifier.
findings.finding: The plan describes a 'composite `TokenVerifier` + config-driven selection' in the Approach but does not specify the routing/composition protocol: is exactly one mode selectable at a time (exclusive), or can multiple verifiers be chained (composite fallback)? The decision rule for how the composite dispatcher routes a token — and what happens when the selected verifier returns `None` vs. raises — is not co-located with any schema definition, leaving a structural gap between the named 'composite' architecture and the per-verifier hardening rules.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('composite `TokenVerifier` + config-driven selection'); Security section ('any verifier error denies')

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
